articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Four more race newspapers
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (May 9, 1914) Continued newspaper survey: The Sun (1892, George W. Gardiner, Rev. William H. Heard associate), The Standard Echo (Apr. 1895, Abel P. Caldwell) with its account of a Frederick Douglass Monumental Association and the Caterers Supply Co. (P. A. Dutrieuille, Walter P. Hall, John S. Trower and others), and Robert G. Still's Sunday Journal with the roster of the Citizens Republican Club (Andrew F. Stevens, president). Notes Robert Purvis was then the only man of his race in the Philadelphia Blue Book.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Relics of early Methodism and the General Conference of 1848
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (Oct. 19, 1912) Urges collecting relics of A.M.E. history before the 1916 centennial — citing Robert M. Adger's discovery of an early portrait of Bishop Richard Allen and the collections at the Home for Aged and Infirm Colored Persons and the American Negro Historical Society. Then details the Eighth General Conference of the A.M.E. Church, Philadelphia, May 1, 1848: Bishop William Paul Quinn presiding, M. M. Clark secretary, A. W. Wayman and A. Davis assistant secretaries, 74 delegates from nine states and Canada. Prominent figures: Daniel A. Payne ('omnipresent'), George Hogarth, John Peck, N. C. W. Cannon, Rev. Stephen Smith, Dr. J. J. G. Bias, Willis Nazrey, David Ware, John Cornish, Peter Gardner, John M. Brown (later bishop), A. R. Green, Walter Proctor, Joshua Woodlyn and others.

events.name: Eighth General Conference of the A.M.E. Church
events.description: 74 delegates from PA, NJ, DE, NY, the Eastern States, MD, OH, IN and Canada; ministers of other denominations invited to seats without vote (recalled in Pencil Pusher Points, Oct. 19, 1912).
